words.en: vitamin B (any of the vitamins in the vitamin B complex)
glosses: vitamin B (any of the vitamins in the vitamin B complex)

words.en: vitamin B complex (vitamins belonging to group B)
glosses: vitamin B complex (vitamins belonging to group B)

words.etymology: From Old Georgian აბანოჲ (abanoy), from Proto-Georgian-Zan *ban- (whence also ბანაობს (banaobs), ბანა (bana) and their derivatives). Cognate with Mingrelian ბონუა (bonua, “to wash oneself”), აბანა (abana) and Laz ობონუ (obonu, “to wash oneself”).

examples.en: tinder fungus (Fomes fomentarius and Phellinus igniarius (syn. Fomes igniarius))

words.etymology: Inherited from Old Georgian აბედი (abedi). Further etymology is unknown.
From Latin absinthium. Replaced Old Georgian აფსინდი (apsindi).

words.en: pill (small portion of a drug or drugs to be taken orally)
glosses: pill (small portion of a drug or drugs to be taken orally)

words.en: college applicant, candidate for qualifying examination for admission to a university
glosses: college applicant, candidate for qualifying examination for admission to a university
words.etymology: From Russian абитурие́нт (abiturijént) or German Abiturient.
From აბონირება (abonireba) + -ული (-uli, past passive participle-forming suffix).

words.en: having white, reddish, brown or black spots.
glosses: having white, reddish, brown or black spots.

words.etymology: From Old Georgian აბრეშუმი (abrešumi), see there for more.
From Old Georgian აბჯარი (abǯari); an Iranian borrowing. See Old Armenian օճառ (ōčaṙ) for more.
Inherited from Old Georgian აგარაკი (agaraḳi), from Old Armenian ագարակ (agarak). For the development of the ending see -აკი (-aḳi).
From Old Georgian აგარაკი (agaraḳi), from Old Armenian ագարակ (agarak).
From Russian агно́стик (agnóstik) + -ოსი (-osi, hellenizing occupational suffix).
From Old Georgian აგური (aguri), see there for more. For the development of the sense “diamonds”, compare Armenian ագուռ (aguṙ, “brick; diamonds”), քյարփինջ (kʻyarpʻinǰ, “brick; diamonds”).

examples.en: This plant is a native species of the Caucasus.

words.etymology: Borrowed from Russian Оде́сса (Odéssa, “Odessa”), whence the grape was introduced into the South Caucasus and Pontus. An oral doublet of ოდესა (odesa).

words.en: a unit of length equal to about 101,15 cm, later displaced by არშინი (aršini)
glosses: a unit of length equal to about 101,15 cm, later displaced by არშინი (aršini)
words.etymology: Ultimately from Arabic عَدْل (ʕadl, “justice; right measure; in numismatics (coin) of just weight; of equal value”). The sense development is comparable to the one that happened in the hapax Middle Armenian ատիլ (atil, “a unit of weight”) from the same source, if it is not a misspelling for աւղիւ (awłiw).
Most probably borrowed from Russian адмира́л (admirál).
From Old Georgian ადრე (adre), from Proto-Georgian-Zan *adr-. Cognate with Mingrelian ორდო (ordo, “morning”), Laz ოდრო (odro), ორდო (ordo, “quick, fast; incessantly”).
From ადრე (adre, “early, before”) + -ული (-uli, adjectival suffix) + -ად (-ad, adverbial suffix).
Borrowed from Russian аэродро́м (aerodróm), itself from French aérodrome.

From Middle Georgian ავაზა (avaza, “cheetah”), from Old Georgian ავაზაჲ (avazay), ავაზი (avazi, “leopard”). From Iranian, however, according to Hübschmann and Ačaṙyan, via Old Armenian յաւազ (yawaz); see there for more.
From Old Georgian ავაზაკი (avazaḳi), which together with Old Armenian աւազակ (awazak) is borrowed from Middle Iranian. See the Armenian entry for more.

words.en: large, elongated trough under a spring collecting water for the cattle
glosses: large, elongated trough under a spring collecting water for the cattle
words.etymology: From Old Georgian ავაზანი (avazani). Doublet of აბაზანა (abazana).
From Old Georgian ჰავი (havi), ავი (avi, “evil, wicked”). Further origin is unknown. Considering the initial ჰ- (h-), the word is most likely borrowed from an outside source.

words.en: air ticket, airline ticket, flight ticket, plane ticket
glosses: air ticket, airline ticket, flight ticket, plane ticket

words.etymology: Borrowed from Russian авиа́тор (aviátor), from French aviateur.
